story.title: Iguanas can drop their tail on purpose 🦎
story.description: A clip of reptiles: an iguana on the tree branch
story.seo_title: Iguanas can drop their tail on purpose 🦎
story.yt_description: Meet the iguana. This lizard does something wild to get away. That tail you see? It's detachable. Source: Pexels
story.hook: An iguana's tail just fell off.
story.script: An iguana's tail just fell off. It did that on purpose, like a trick. Watch the eyes—it’s already planning its escape. That’s why predators get a mouthful of tail while the iguana dashes away. How would you fake your own death to survive?
story.lead: An iguana's tail just fell off. It did that on purpose, like a trick. Watch the eyes—it’s already planning its escape. That’s why predators get a mouthful of tail while the iguana dashes away. How would you fake your own death to survive?

story.title: Armadillos hunt by sound while blind — here's how
story.description: A clip of nocturnal animals: armadillo foraging in natural habitat footage
story.seo_title: Armadillos hunt by sound while blind — here's how
story.yt_description: Armadillos forage at night. They close their eyes when they dig so they can focus on listening for grubs. Source: Pexels
story.script: Armadillos hunt by sound alone. They close their eyes while digging so nothing distracts their ears. Watch the nose twitch—each flick is a grubs location check. That’s why they don’t need to see underground. Ever wonder how they find food so fast? Comment below.
story.lead: Armadillos hunt by sound alone. They close their eyes while digging so nothing distracts their ears. Watch the nose twitch—each flick is a grubs location check. That’s why they don’t need to see underground. Ever wonder how they find food so fast? Comment below.
